data.title: Gasoline Declines to Early-April Lows
data.description:
US gasoline futures fell below $2 per gallon, hitting early-April lows as ample supply and moderating demand weighed on the market.

Prices eased on expectations of reduced US fuel consumption with the end of the summer driving season after Labor Day, compounded by concerns of a global glut after the IEA projected supply will outpace demand in the coming quarters and OPEC+ moved to restore idled capacity.

Meanwhile, gasoline inventories, which had briefly tightened on pre-holiday demand surges, have since stabilized, while refinery utilization remains strong, ensuring steady fuel availability.

With demand cooling after the August peak and additional crude output anticipated from OPEC+, the supply-demand balance has shifted decisively in favor of lower pump prices.

data.title: SentinelOne Price Target Maintained With a $29.00/Share by JMP Securities
Euronext wheat steadies after lows
data.description:
European wheat futures edged up on Friday to recover from contract lows, supported by a rally in Chicago corn, traders said.

Large global supplies, tepid international demand and signs of accelerating exports from Russia limited gains, however.

December milling wheat (BL2Z5) on Paris-based Euronext settled 0.8% higher at 194.00 euros ($227.12) per metric ton. On Thursday, it had set a contract low of 191.75 euros that also marked the weakest second-month price (BL2c2) since March 2024.

September wheat (BL2U5), which expires on September 10, ended 1.1% higher at 191.50 euros a ton, moving away from Thursday's contract low of 188.00 euros.

Chicago wheat was also firm, helped by a sharp rise for U.S. corn that was boosted in turn by strong exports and positioning before the Labor Day holiday weekend in the United States.

In Europe, traders were assessing Russia's increasing competitiveness after a sluggish start to its wheat export season.

“The supply outlook is looking larger with new-crop exports by Russia gradually increasing after a very slow start and Russian prices declining, although slowly,” one German trader said.

Market estimates were putting Russian exports so far in August at well above 3 million tons, including 700,000 tons to Egypt, the trader added.

Consultancy Sovecon has increased its estimate of Russian August exports to 4 million tons, double the July volume.

Russian 12.5% protein September wheat was on Friday around $232-$234 a ton FOB. Russian 11.5% protein wheat was around $227 to $231 a ton FOB, about $2 cheaper than French depending on Euronext and exchange rate moves.

In Poland, the rain-disrupted harvest is finishing, with traders estimating about 30% of the crop will only reach feed quality, like in Germany.

A ship is loading about 55,000 tons of Polish feed wheat in Gdynia for an unusual sale to Vietnam, traders said, in a sign of export demand for lower-grade wheat.

Poland was nonetheless expected to have a milling wheat export surplus of 5 million tons out of an expected harvest of 13.5 million tons, one Polish trader said.

“Despite the high volume of feed wheat Poland is still likely to have enough milling wheat for domestic and export needs,” the trader said.

"But the concern is where will Poland export to when competition from the Black Sea looks like being so hard.”

($1 = 0.8542 euros)

data.title: Sea Forrest International Seeks Valuation of Up to $64.4M in IPO
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Sea Forrest International is seeking a valuation of up to $64.4 million in its initial public offering.

The Singapore holding company--which operates through subsidiaries that provide afloat repairs and green-energy solutions--will offer nearly 3.8 million common shares, expecting to sell each for between $4 and $4.50, according to a Friday filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Following the offering, Sea Forrest would have about 13.8 million shares outstanding, or roughly 14.3 million shares outstanding if underwriters fully exercise their option to buy added shares.

Sea Forrest expects net proceeds of $12.8 million from the offering, assuming it sells $15 million worth of its common shares. The company intends to use proceeds for geographical business expansion, marketing development and trade shows, research and development, facility expansions and working capital.

Sea Forrest posted a net loss of $857.5 million on revenue of $5.08 billion in 2024. It plans to list shares on the Nasdaq under the ticker SFTI.

Maxim Group is listed as the offering's underwriter.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Platinum Hits 4-week High
data.description:
Platinum increased to 1367.00 USD/t.oz, the highest since July 2025.

Over the past 4 weeks, Platinum gained 4.47%, and in the last 12 months, it increased 47.61%.

data.title: Spain's BBVA tweaks bid for Sabadell following dividend payment
data.description:
Spain's BBVA said on Friday it had adjusted its takeover offer for Sabadell to maintain its value after the latter's interim dividend payment, as it awaits the supervisor's approval for its now over 15 billion euro ($17.56 billion) hostile bid.

Following Sabadell's payment on Friday of a 0.07 euro interim dividend against 2025 results, BBVA is now offering one newly issued ordinary share and 0.70 euros in cash for every 5.5483 ordinary shares of Sabadell, it said in a filing, compared to a previous exchange ratio of 5.3456.

Combining the two lenders would create a bank with more than 1 trillion euros in total assets and mark the latest consolidation move in Spain's banking industry.

In April of 2024, BBVA announced a more than 12 billion euro bid for all Sabadell's shares, which turned hostile in May.

As BBVA shares have risen to 15.490 euros as of Friday's closing from 10.90 euros when the offer was first made, the offer is now worth around 15.5 billion euros, taking into account the new number of shares BBVA would have to issue now based on the new exchange ratio, Reuters calculations show.

Sabadell's shares already trade above the 30% premium to the April 29, 2024 closing price originally offered by BBVA, and have since outperformed BBVA shares.

BBVA is now waiting for the supervisor's approval before formally kicking off its acceptance period and bringing the bid directly to Sabadell shareholders.

($1 = 0.8542 euros)

data.title: Super Micro shares dip after AI server maker flags financial control concerns
data.description:
Super Micro Computer's shares fell nearly 5% on Friday after the artificial intelligence-optimized server maker reiterated weaknesses in internal control over financial reporting.

The company disclosed in a regulatory filing on Thursday that unresolved issues could "adversely affect" its ability to report results of operations in a timely and accurate manner.

In its annual report for the fiscal year ended June 30, Super Micro repeated similar statements from its May quarterly filing, adding that it was working to address the problems.

Last year, the San Jose, California-based company missed an August deadline to file its annual financial report and Ernst & Young LLP resigned as its auditor in October, citing concerns about governance and transparency.

Super Micro had filed its long-delayed annual report earlier this year.

The company is set to lose over $1 billion from its market value of about $26 billion, if Friday's losses hold.

Super Micro trades at 16.28 times the estimates of its earnings for the next 12 months, compared with 13.12 times for Dell and 10.81 times for Hewlett Packard Enterprise .

Among the 19 brokerages covering Super Micro, seven rate the stock "buy", nine "hold" and three "sell", with a median price target of $49, according to data compiled by LSEG.

Meanwhile, shares of rival Dell fell about 10% on Friday as high manufacturing costs for AI-driven servers and rising competition offset its bullish demand forecast for AI infrastructure.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 1 PM ET: Consumer Spending Comes In Strong as Inflation Data Point to Rate Cut | U.S. ...
data.description:
Consumer Spending Comes In Strong as Inflation Data Point to Rate Cut

The core personal consumption expenditures price index, which excludes food and energy prices, rose 2. 9% year over year, the biggest increase since February.

----

U.S. trade deficit in goods widened sharply in July as firms raced to avoid tariffs

The U.S. trade deficit in goods widened 22.1% to $103.6 billion in July from $84.9 billion, according to the Commerce Department's advance estimate released Friday.

----

Nasdaq Composite Leads Major Indexes Downward on AI Worries

U.S. stocks are poised to end August on a downbeat note, with market favorite Nvidia and other AI-linked stocks leading a broad retreat.

----

India's Economic Growth Unexpectedly Picks Up Speed

Official data Friday showed that India's economy grew 7.8% from a year earlier, up from 7.4% in the prior quarter.

----

Eurozone's Big Economies Show Few Signs of Rising Inflation

The results support the slim possibility of a fresh cut to interest rates next month.

----

Senate Democrats Warn Trump IPO Plan for Fannie, Freddie Could Push Up Mortgage Rates

Senators including Elizabeth Warren urge housing officials to focus on affordability.

----

Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Jobs Data in Focus as Fed Rate Cut Looks Likely

U.S. jobs data, alongside the latest ISM surveys on manufacturing and services, will provide indications on how the economy is faring after weak jobs data for July.

----

Higher Prices Are Coming for Household Staples

Companies from Hormel to Ace Hardware are forecasting rising prices as the costs of President Trump's tariffs are passed on to consumers.

----

Trump Leans on National Security to Justify Next Wave of Tariffs

The administration plans to expand a certain category of tariffs in hopes of redirecting production to the U.S. and thwarting potential legal threats in the trade war.

----

Fed's Waller backs a series of interest-rate cuts over the next six months

Federal Reserve governor Christopher Waller on Thursday said he now supports a series of interest-rate cuts beginning in September, with the pace of subsequent moves driven by the incoming data.

----

Fed Governor Lisa Cook Sues to Stop Trump From Firing Her

The lawsuit tees up a high-stakes court battle over the president's authority to remove members of the central bank's board.

----

U.S. Economy Grew Faster in Second Quarter, New Estimate Shows

Consumer spending and AI investment add to the revision in quarterly GDP.

----

New Tariffs on Low-Cost Imports Could Bring in $10 Billion a Year

Some countries have temporarily halted the so-called de minimis shipments in anticipation of the rule change.

----

Trump's push for lower interest rates is on a collision course with government bonds

Bond investors are taking a "wait-and-see" approach to President Donald Trump's attempt to fire Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook.

----

E.U. to Scrap Duties on U.S. Industrial Goods as It Scrambles to Soften Auto Tariff Blow

The European Union will move to eliminate all tariffs on U.S. industrial imports and expand access for American farm products, part of an effort to shield European automakers from steeper duties on their exports to the U.S.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 1 PM ET: DuPont to Sell Kevlar, Nomex Business to Arclin for $1.8 Billion | Whitney ...
data.description:
DuPont to Sell Kevlar, Nomex Business to Arclin for $1.8 Billion

The sale of Aramids is the latest in DuPont's decadelong transformation, in which spinoffs and sales have left it a fraction of its former size.

----

Whitney Wolfe Herd Has a New Idea for Bumble-and All Our Relationships

She's spent nearly all of her working life atop the dating-app business, and believes artificial intelligence could be the matchmaker we all need.

----

PepsiCo Boosting Stake in Celsius in Deal Worth $585 Million

PepsiCo is boosting its stake in energy-drink maker Celsius Holdings in a deal worth $585 million as the companies expand their three-year partnership.

----

Alibaba Creates AI Chip to Help China Fill Nvidia Void

Chinese chip companies and artificial-intelligence developers are building up their arsenal of homegrown technology, backed by a government determined to win the AI race.

----

Sorry Europe, American Luxury Brands Are In

High-end U.S. fashion houses are outperforming in shops and in the stock market.

----

BYD's First-Half Profit Rose, Margin Narrowed

The Chinese auto giant's results come amid concerns the company won't meet its sales target for the year.

----

Cambricon Technologies Warns of Trading Risks After Stock Surges

The chip maker overtook liquor maker Kweichow Moutai as China's most valuable stock this week.

----

Ulta Beauty Lifts Fiscal-Year Guidance as Second-Quarter Profit, Sales Rise

The cosmetics and fragrances retailer boosted its guidance for the fiscal year.

----

Gap Expects Stronger Headwinds From Tariffs This Fiscal Year

The apparel company forecast larger ripples from tariffs this fiscal year given recent changes in trade policy, but said it expects to mitigate any added costs over time.

----

Dell Technologies Raises Full-Year Outlook, Third-Quarter View is Mixed

The company raised its outlook for the year as AI demand rages on, though softer expectations for the current quarter weighed on the stock.

----

Caterpillar Says Higher Tariff Costs May Reach $1.8 Billion

The construction and mining manufacturer is now expecting tariffs to have a greater impact on its finances.

----

Petco Boosts Earnings Outlook, Swings to Profit

The pet-products retailer raised its full-year earnings outlook after swinging to a profit in its latest quarter as its turnaround progresses.

----

Autodesk Bumps Up Guidance as Sales Jump

The company, which provides AutoCAD and other design software, logged double-digit sales growth in its latest quarter, helped by strong demand for its architecture, engineering, construction and operations software.

data.title: More Shrimp Recalled from Kroger, Walmart for Radioactive Exposure
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Shrimp sold at Kroger and Walmart has been recalled for potential contamination with the radioactive isotope cesium-137.

The Food and Drug Administration updated an announcement on Friday, saying Southwind Foods, Beaver Street Fisheries and Aquastar had recalled frozen shrimp products processed by PT Bahari Makmur Sejati, an Indonesian company doing business as BMS Foods.

The recalls include 26,460 packages of cocktail shrimp sold at Walmart stores between July 31 and Aug. 16, and 18,000 bags of Kroger's Mercado Frozen Cooked Shrimp sold at various grocery stores between July 24 and Aug. 11.

The primary health effect from cesium-137 exposure is an elevated risk of cancer. No product has tested positive for cesium-137 so far, the FDA said.

Consumers who purchased the shrimp should not consume it, and should throw it away, the FDA said.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Japanese Lifestyle Entertainment Company TryHard Closes IPO
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

TryHard Holdings closed its previously announced initial public offering.

The Japanese lifestyle entertainment company said it received net proceeds of about $2.7 million from the offering of 1.53 million shares.

Shares began trading Thursday on the Nasdaq under the symbol THH.

TryHard plans to use the proceeds for business development and marketing, acquisitions and working capital.

The underwriters of the IPO exercised their 45-day option to purchase 228,750 shares to cover over-allotments, the Osaka, Japan, company said.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com

data.title: U.S. Road Travel Saw Year-to-Year Increases in May, June, Federal Data Show — OPIS
data.description:
Travel by U.S. motorists saw year-to-year increases in both May and June, according to recently released data from the Federal Highway Administration.

Travel on all roads and streets totaled 294 billion vehicle miles in May, up 0.5% from the same month in 2024. Travel in June totaled 285.6 billion miles, up 0.9% from the same time last year, according to the FHA Traffic Volume Trends report.

Seasonally adjusted data, which attempts to compensate for differences in the numbers of days in a month, holidays and the differences in makeup of days of the week for each month, saw a year-to-year increase of 0.8% in May to 275.4 billion miles and 0.7% increase in June to 274.9 billion miles.

Seasonally adjusted travel in May was 0.5% lower than in April, while travel in June was 0.1% lower when compared to May, according to FHA data.

Cumulative travel for 2025 was estimated to be 1.3375 trillion miles, up 0.7% from the same period last year while the cumulative total for June was 1.623 trillion miles, a 0.8% increase over the same time last year, according to the FHA.

The federal agency divides the U.S. into five regions and reports that travel rose on a year-to-year basis in four of the five regions in May. Travel in the North-East region is estimated at 38.7 billion miles, down 1.2% from the previous year. The largest regional increase was a 1.2% year-to-year change in the South-Gulf region to 62.5 billion miles, followed by a 1.1% increase in the West to 63.1 billion miles. Travel increased 0.5% in the South-Atlantic to 64.3 billion miles and by 0.4% to 65.5 billion miles in the North-Central region.

Travel in the North-East also saw a year-to-year decrease in June, falling 0.3% to 38.5 billion miles. The West saw the largest regional increase during the month, with travel rising by 1.2% to 62.8 billion miles, followed by a 1.1% increase in the South-Atlantic to 61.5 billion miles. Travel increased by 1% in the North-Central region to 63.6 billion miles while in the South-Gulf the year-to-year increase was 0.9% to 59.2 billion mile, the FHA reported.

While the FHA normally releases the traffic volume data monthly, the May and June data were released together on Thursday.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

  • Reporting by Steve Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com; Editing by Michael Kelly, mkelly@opisnet.com

data.title: Affirm's Reliance on Big Merchants Seen as Risk Factor — Market Talk
data.description: Affirm beat expectations in its fiscal fourth quarter, but UBS analysts worry it may be too reliant on just a few big merchants. The analysts are keeping their neutral rating for the stock because of how concentrated its business is on a few merchants as buy now, pay later competition is rising from rivals such as Klarna and Paypal. Amazon and Shopify contributed around 35% of Affirm's gross merchandise volume in the fourth quarter, the analysts say. Affirm lost Walmart as one of its biggest merchants earlier this year when the retail giant switched to Klarna. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Canada Economy Contracts in Second Quarter as Tariffs Sink Exports — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada's economy was hit hard by the ramp up in tariffs in the latest quarter, when a slump in exports and drop in business investment led to the sharpest contraction in activity since early in the pandemic.

The economy softened each month through the April-to-June period as trade-exposed sectors of the economy in particular struggled. And early data for July suggests Canada may struggle to find growth over the remainder of the year, heating up the debate over whether the central bank will resume cutting interest rates as soon as its next policy meeting.

The quarterly fall was steeper than the 0.7% contraction economists were expecting, though closer to the 1.5% retreat projected by the Bank of Canada at the time of its last meeting in late July, when it again held its benchmark rate steady.

Adding to the soft picture for the nation, the expansion in the first three months of the year was revised lower to 2.0% annualized from an earlier estimate of 2.2% growth, and industry-level GDP unexpectedly edged down 0.1% in June from the month before to match the pullback in activity in each of the previous two months. Statistics Canada's flash estimate for July pointed to a modest 0.1% rise in GDP.

Canadian government bond yields fell following the data, signaling that investors are pricing in a greater chance of a rate cut, with money markets predicting a nearly 50% chance of a cut at the central bank's Sept. 17 meeting against a roughly 40% chance before the GDP numbers.

The fall in quarterly GDP, a broad measure of goods and services produced across the country, was the first in seven quarters and the steepest decline since the second quarter of 2020, when activity sank 37% during the Covid-19 lockdowns.

The squeeze came with the escalation in tariffs by the Trump administration and a reversal of the demand early in the year when shipments to the U.S. jumped amid tariff front-running by businesses. Second-quarter exports sank an annualized 26.8% and knocked 9.8 percentage points off of GDP, mitigated only somewhat by a comparatively softer decline in imports that added 3.2 points to GDP.

Investment by businesses in machinery and equipment was also down steeply, falling just over 10% on an annual basis for the weakest result since the end of 2016, outside of the first year of the pandemic.

"The tariff war with the U.S. was terrible for the Canadian economy," said Royce Mendes, head of macroeconomic strategy at Desjardins, who continues to predict interest rate cuts in September and beyond.

Tariffs and uncertainty are expected to weigh on Canadian economic activity for some time, though economists in recent months have dropped their earlier more-pessimistic calls on the economy since most of the goods shipped to the U.S. have been exempted from tariffs so long as they comply with the existing North American trade agreement. Still, polls show businesses and households remain wary. And President Trump has threatened another wave of tariffs that expand national-security levies on imports including steel and aluminum, much of which comes from Canada.

"The Canadian economy has shown greater resilience than initially expected and both business and consumer confidence have rebounded from earlier lows" David McKay, chief executive of Royal Bank of Canada, told investors Thursday. He said that if goods compliant with the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement continue to qualify for tariff exemption in the U.S., then Canada's effective tariff rate should remain low and the economy is likely to remain resilient.

USMCA compliance and Ottawa's decision to lower Canadian counter tariffs are helping to cushion the fallout from the trade war on Canada's economy, and it looks increasingly likely that GDP may avoid another contraction in the third quarter, said Michael Davenport, senior economist at Oxford Economics. "However, the impact of the trade war and elevated uncertainty on sentiment, capital spending and hiring will likely continue to build. We expect the economy will struggle to grow in the second half and teeter on the verge of recession."

There were bright spots in the latest data. Final consumption was up strongly after a soft start to the year. Household spending climbed 4.5% annualized from the prior quarter, and government expenditure was up 5.1% after shrinking slightly in the first quarter.

These parts of the data hint at a considerably stronger domestic economy than headline GDP indicates, though some economists question whether the trend can be sustained. Final domestic demand, which strips out the effects of trade and inventories and looks at consumption, investment and government spending, was up 3.4% annualized for the quarter.

While some economists suggest that could temper any need for the central bank to lower rates in a hurry, others believe policymakers are more likely to be swayed by indications there was little momentum for the economy into the third quarter.

Statistics Canada's early estimate for industry-level accounts suggests GDP in July increased 0.1% on the month before, with increases in real estate, mining and wholesale trade. That would follow a surprise 0.1% decline in June GDP, where economists were expecting growth of 0.1%, after a similar pace of contraction in both April and May, as manufacturing continued to be hit hard. The agency noted that two-fifths of manufacturers in June reported being affected by tariffs.

"The upshot is GDP [growth] is tracking a disappointing 0.5% this quarter, undershooting the Bank of Canada's forecast and making a September cut look a bit more likely...though August's labor-market data due for release next week could shift the picture quickly," said Capital Economics' North America economist Thomas Ryan.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: VAT announces change in the Group Executive Committee
data.description:
VAT Group AG / Key word(s): Personnel

29-Aug-2025 / 18:45 CET/CEST

Release of an ad hoc announcement pursuant to Art. 53 LR

The issuer is solely responsible for the content of this announcement.

Finn Felsberg, Executive Vice President of the Semiconductor Solutions Group (SSG) and a member of the Group Executive Committee, is leaving VAT to pursue opportunities outside the company.

Finn joined VAT in December 2023 and in this capacity has been responsible for the Group’s Semiconductor business including the core valve activities, the adjacency business and the corporate R&D function. During his tenure, VAT has further strengthened its leading product and market position, especially in the core valve business while at the same time capturing substantial additional business opportunities in adjacent products and services. He also focused on the development of a strong and agile internal mindset regarding additional future business areas along the vacuum chain.

We thank Finn for his commitment, dedication and his valuable contribution to the success of VAT and wish him success for his future.

Effective immediately and until the succession planning is concluded, Urs Gantner, CEO and former EVP of the Semiconductor Solutions Group, will lead the semiconductor business activities.

  • For further information please contact:

    VAT Group AG

    Michel R. Gerber

    Head of Sustainability and Investor Relations

    T +41 81 553 70 13

    investors@vatgroup.com
